details.text: Derivative: كَيْفَ (Kayfa) means “how” and refers to the manner of how something happened or is done. The focus here is therefore not on what Allah did but rather “how” He did it emphasising the power of Allah in a more elaborate manner. كَيْفَ (Kayfa) can also imply amazement and wonder i.e., “How could that have happened like that?!”

Derivative: فَعَلَ – فِعْل which mean “to do” and “act” is used for to refer to an action that has a tangible impact. The word فِعْلًا (Fi’lan) or phrase بِالفِعْل (Bil Fi’l) means “really” or “actually” i.e., something that is tangible and actually exists or takes place. The word فَعَلَ is associated with Allah in the Qur’an whilst عَمِلَ which also means “to do or undertake an act” is not. This can be due to فَعَلَ requiring less effort than عَمِلَ which highlights that what Allah does is easy and effortless for Him.

Derivative: رَبُّ (Rabb - Lord and Master). When الله (Allah) is used it refers to all of Allah’s qualities. When رَبُّ (Rabb) is used the main attribute that is highlighted is that He is the Master who has slaves. The use implies that the audience should enslave themselves to their Rabb.

Derivative: أَلَمْ تَرَ كَيْفَ “Did you not see how?!” implies تَعْجِيب (Ta’jeeb) which means to arouse and stir wonder and amazement i.e., “Did you not see how such an incredible and amazing thing happened?!”
- It can also act as a reminder and warning i.e., Have you not seen how Allah deals with His enemies!”
- The question form with the use of the Hamzah أَ can also imply that the Quraish are being reminded of a favour “Do you not consider what your Lord did when He helped and saved you in such an incredible way not long ago?!” It can also be a reminder for the Messenger ﷺ that Allah will also come to his help despite bad odds or weak means.

- أَلَمْ تَرَ “Did you not see?” is a figure of speech in Arabic. تَرَ “You see” is in the singular and this implies that it could be addressing the Messenger ﷺ or that it is addressing each person individually to reflect upon.
- تَرَ from رَأَى – يَرَى can mean to physically see something and also to figuratively see in the sense of to “consider”, “regard” or “contemplate or think about” something. أَلَمْ تَرَ would therefore mean: “Did you not consider or contemplate about?”
- Even though أَلَمْ تَرَ “Did you not see?” alludes to the past and to a specific incident, the use of the مُضَارِع (Mudaari’) present tense form تَرَ can imply continuity and that this incident can be applied to other incidents as well i.e., it is not just something to consider, contemplate and reflect upon for that time but rather in all times.
- أَلَمْ تَرَ “Did you not see?” even though it happened before implies knowledge and a reminder like “Do you not know?” and “Can you not remember?” or “Have you forgotten?”
- أَلَمْ is used often in the form of a rebuke and warning in the Qur’an: “Did you not see?!” Do you not know?!” etc.

- “The people of (أَصْحَابِ) the elephant (الْفِيلِ)”. The people are being associated with the elephant which is singular even though there were many elephants involved in the incident. The association is therefore being made with the most notable elephant in the incident. فِيل can also be اِسْم الجَمْع (Ism ul-Jam’) which is noun that appears similar but applies to the collective category or species i.e., to mean elephants in general. The meaning in that case would be “The people of the elephants”. The general meaning is that these people were identified by an army which included elephants.
- The association by calling them the people of the elephants has also been said to imply condescension towards them and belittling them by associating them with animals rather than any other attribute. This is reiterated by the fact that in Arabic when صاحِب or its plural أَصْحَاب is used to imply companionship and association, it is normal for the one of higher standing to be stated second, however here they are being mentioned as being the companions of the elephant, placing the elephant at a higher level of status than them.

Derivative: يَجْعَلْ is from جَعَلَ and means to take something that already exists and to transform it. Here in respect to their كَيْدَ (plot and scheme) the use of the verb indicates that Allah allowed them to make their plot and carry it out until the very end and then at that point He caused it to go to waste and fail completely i.e., he pulled the rug from under their feet.
Derivative: كَيْدَ refers to a plot or plan that is hatched in secret and its aim or intent is to bring some kind of harm to someone or undermine them.

Derivative: تَضْلِيلٍ comes from ضَلَّلَ – يُضَلِّلُ – تَضْلِيلًا. ضَلَّ means to be lost, to lose one’s way or go astray. ضَلَّلَ means to make something lost where it is laid to waste, made to utterly fail and can no long be used at all. تَضْلِيلٍ is in the تَفْعِيل pattern form which can imply repetition i.e., the plot was made to fail on more than one occasion or in more than one attempt.

أَلَمْ يَجْعَلْ كَيْدَهُمْ فِي تَضْلِيلٍ

- “Did He not (أَلَمْ) make (يَجْعَلْ) there plot (كَيْدَهُمْ) go to waste (فِي تَضْلِيلٍ)”. The use of the present tense in the word يَجْعَلْ indicates to continuity i.e., that this is something that Allah does time and time again.
- the use of فِي (in) before تَضْلِيلٍ (waste) here illustrates that their كَيْدَ (plot) was in a state of being laid to waste implying that it was completely laid to waste.

Derivative: أَبَابِيلَ (Abaabeel) means large groups upon groups, or flocks upon flocks. There is no singular for it. It has been said to refer to large groups that follow one after the other consecutively.

Derivative: طَيْرًا is an اِسْمُ الجَمْعِ (Ism ul-Jam’) a collective plural for a category or species meaning birds as a general category including all its varieties. The تَنْوِين (Tanween) in طَيْرًا instead of using the definite article of ال can be used for the purpose of demonstrating تَنْوِيع (Tanwee’ - variety of different kinds) and for the purpose of تَهِوِيل (Tahweel – to induce horror or terror) because the تَنْوِين (Tanween) is used in Arabic to demonstrate condemnation and to incite something.

- “He sent (أَرْسَلَ) upon them (عَلَيْهِمْ)” the use of عَلَى “upon” implies coming from high which is indicative of dominance and supremacy. أَرْسَلَ عَلَى “He sent upon” is also the Qur’anic expression which used when a punishment is sent upon a people i.e., a punishment that is sent down and descends upon them or is sent against them.

Derivative: سِجِّيلٍ has had different explanations provided for it. One is that the word is of Persian origin and was adopted by the Arabs. It refers to pebbles which are formed on the ground from the rain and mud which then dry up and harden. It has also been described as the kind of stone that is extremely solid and penetrates or pierces the body. This meaning conjures an image that is like bullets that enter and pierce through a body. A common understanding among the scholars of Tafsir is that سِجِّيلٍ refers to pebbles that have been baked and burned by the sun from clay.
Derivative: حِجَارَةٍ is the plural of حَجَر and means stones.

Derivative: تَرْمِي is from رَمَى – يَرْمِي – رَمْي which means to throw. تَرْمِي is the present tense (المُضارِع – Al-Mudaari’). When the present tense is used to refer to something in the past, the purpose is to demonstrate that it happened over and over again and as such تَرْمِي implies that the birds kept throwing. رَمْي means to throw from a distance and also implies that the throwing is being aimed at a specific target.

Derivative: عَصْفٍ – عَصَفَ means to storm and rage and is used for wind. A wind that is عَاصِف (‘Aasif) is one that blows violently and shakes everything up. عَصْفٍ refers to the fragments of leaves, straws and other parts of crops that are left upon the ground after the harvesting of the crops and the winds throw them around.
Derivative: كَ is a حَرْف (letter) of تَشْبِيه (Tashbeeh – to draw a similarity, resemblance or comparison) which is used to imply likeness i.e., something is like something.

Derivative: جَعَلَ means to take something that already exists and to transform it, like taking wood and transforming or making it into a table. Here, they were a powerful army before and then they were transformed and made into something else.

- عَصْفٍ مَّأْكُولٍ – مَّأْكُولٍ means “eaten” and عَصْفٍ مَّأْكُول was a phrase that the Arabs used to refer to the fragments or remnants of straw that would fall out of the sides of the mouth of a camel when it would eat after being chewed up, crushed and grinded. It is also said to refer to the leaves and fragments of crops that are blown around by the winds and eaten by insects or animals. مَّأْكُولٍ “to be eaten” can also imply something that is supposed to be eaten and devoured implying that it is helpless and has no escape from being eaten.
